title: Rewriting the Futhark type checker
excerpt: A high-performance and high-level purely functional data-parallel array programming language that can execute on the GPU and CPU.
content_html:
Posted on July 21, 2026

This post is about the evolution of Futhark’s type checker, motivated by a large refactoring I am about to merge. It is probably mostly of interest to other language designers, and contains some lessons I wish I had known when we first got started - although I am not particularly well-read in the type checking literature, so it’s possible all of this is old hat.

Background

Far back in the primordial era when we first designed Futhark, the type system was simple: The only types were scalars, arrays, and tuples, all functions were first-order and monomorphic, and there was no type inference. All functions had to be annotated with their parameter and return types. The only fancy feature that was present from the (near) beginning was in-place updates via a kind of uniqueness types, although at the time we didn’t really understand how tricky they were.

At that point it really was just a type checker: starting from the facts that we were told (the types of parameters), we checked if the types of expressions were consistent, and tracked aliases to validate the use of in-place updates. The implementation was a straightforward top-down single traversal of each function in turn, during which AST were decorated nodes with their determined type, which was then used by subsequent passes. It was the exact same kind of type checker that I had been taught (and since taught myself) in a standard course on programming language implementation, and when you can get away with it, this is a good design: easy to implement, pretty efficient, easy to understand. At the time we really didn’t understand the complexity of correctly checking the rules for safe use of in-place updates, so the simplicity was perhaps illusory, but we were happy in those days nonetheless.

Of course, we didn’t get away with it for long. We eventually started adding features such as records and an ML-style module system, and by version 0.4.0 we had put in higher-order functions and Hindley-Milner style type inference. The implementation was a classic (some might say “old-fashioned”) implementation of Algorithm W; the standard Hindley-Milner type inference algorithm. During traversal of a function, we immediately solved type constraints. However, we had some type system features that were never a natural fit with this approach. Both our record system, and also our uniqueness types, depended on being able to query the current type of some program fragment and make decisions based on it. This would show up in cases like branches, where the aliases of if a then b else c depended on whether the type of b and c can carry aliases (primitives cannot). We managed to put together an implementation that worked by putting various extra information into our representation of type variables, but it was a little complicated, not obviously correct, and somewhat tedious to debug.

Later, when we added proper size types, we did it by extending the basic Hindley-Milner system by essentially also treating sizes as a kind of “type” subject to unification. At this point the system was beginning to creak. Consider again a branch if a then b else c - what is the size of the result? If b and c are arrays, we need to determine whether they have the same size, and if not, generate an existential for the result size. But at the point where we are looking at this if, we may not yet know whether they are arrays! Many similar cases occurred, and the type system had various restrictions and quirks where the well-typedness of a function would depend in somewhat subtle ways on whether you had put in type annotations. One nice property however was that if you put in type annotations on all parameters, which is generally considered good style anyway, then things worked pretty predictably. But still, the type checker was not particularly fun to hack on at this point.

Eventually, this design - a single program traversal manipulating an increasingly complicated state - stopped being workable. This was when we started working on AUTOMAP, a (not yet finished) system that loosely means that instead of saying map f x, you can just say f x and have the compiler figure out how many maps are needed. During this work, we discovered that AUTOMAP had to work by taking a global view of a function, typechecking it partially but without inferring specific sizes of arrays, determining the smallest number of maps needed to make it typecheck (by using an ILP solver), and then determine specific sizes. There was no way to fit this design into the existing type checker, so I began taking it apart into multiple phases.

The first phase I extracted was name resolution. It does what you would expect: resolves every name in the program, in practice by assigning each a unique identifier. This was a small win, but it simplified the type checker a little. The next phase was the conceptually final one: checking for aliases and in-place update violations. This turned out to be much more profitable, as this machinery had always been hindered by having to work with incomplete type information. Now it would be given a fully well-typed program and simply had to figure out whether any of the in-place constraints were violated (essentially, whether in-place updates are semantically observable).

We were now left with a remaining core piece: a “term type checker” that did both normal ML-style type inference, but also size inference. As an expediency, to make the AUTOMAP research project progress, I did a hacky thing: I wrote an entirely new constraint-based type checker that did not perform size inference, but rather only standard type inference. We call this the unsized type checker. Instead of solving type equations on the fly, it generated a set of type constraints (mostly type equalities), which were solved “offline” by a constraint solver. This “generate-and-solve” approach makes handling complicated type system features more tractable as you don’t have to make decisions based on partial information, and is also used by the Glasgow Haskell Compiler and the Flix. In our implementation, we used these constraints to drive the AUTOMAP solver, then threw away those constraints and re-ran the original size-aware type checker (along with a bit of extra information provided by AUTOMAP). This worked, in that all programs could type check, but I think our paper puts it generously when it says “our Automap-enabled type checker is unoptimized”. My concern was not merely the inefficiency of redundant work, but the lack of clarity by duplicated logic - every type checking rule was duplicated in code!

The new type checker

I still have some hope for AUTOMAP itself, but getting it in a workable state requires an unknown amount of implementation effort. However, during this work I had become attracted to the elegance of constraint-based type checking. The central advantage is that you can postpone decisions until all information is available. From a human perspective, it is also much easier to debug (or benchmark) when you can print out all the constraints and reason about them. Unfortunately, our implementation was abominable. Last year I started work on doing constraint-based type checking without AUTOMAP, and in particular where duplication of logic was minimised. The idea was to use the unsized type checker to infer “ground” types (meaning types without filling in the specific sizes of arrays), then do a second pass that performs only size inference.

The main obstacle, and the reason I didn’t finish until now, stemmed from tricky cases related to existential sizes, where existential quantifiers of array sizes must be inserted in just the right place. Consider a function with size-lifted type variables like this:

def apply 'a '^b (f: a -> b) (x: a) =
  f x

Since ^b is size-lifted, it can be instantiated with a type that contains existential quantifiers, in this case meaning that f can be a function that returns an array of an unknown size.

If we say apply iota, then the unsized type checker will infer this type for apply:

(f: i64 -> []i64) -> (x: i64) -> []i64 =

When we perform size inference, it is quite important that we infer this type, with existential quantification (?[n]) in return types:

(f: i64 -> ?[n].[n]i64) -> (x: i64) -> ?[m].[m]i64

Rather than let the return size float all the way to the top as a size parameter:

(f: i64 -> [n]i64) -> (x: i64) -> [n]i64

This incorrect type states that the array returned by f is known before f is invoked at all, and does not depend on its parameter. This is true for some fs, but not when f=iota.

In the old type checker, this was done with some clever (oh no…) programming deep in the middle of unification whenever it encountered flexible size-lifted type variables. Since the unsized type checker has now determined all ground types in advance, no flexible type variables are ever encountered, so I had to work out a different solution. The details are too intricate to get into here, but they are ultimately a more explicit handling of the issue. There are still some ugly parts where I think we never worked out a good theory for size inference, but the part of the code that they infect is now a lot smaller than it used to be. I still have a vague idea that we should redo all of size checking at one point, perhaps basing it on bidirectional type checking instead of full inference, since it really is a dependent type system.

But that is for the future. With this work, the Futhark type checker now goes through four stages for each function:

  1. Resolve names.
  2. Determine ground types with the unsized type checker.
  3. Perform size inference.
  4. Check for in-place update violations.

(If we ever finish AUTOMAP, it will go between stages 2 and 3.)

One lovely property of this design is that if an error is encountered in stage i, we have full information from stage i-1 to help explain the error. This is a nice change from the original single-pass type checker, where if something went wrong, we would usually have no information about those parts of the program that we have yet to traverse. I suspect a general principle that it’s a good idea to design a type system in “layers”, with each adding more restrictions to the former, such that a program’s meaning can be understood even when it is not fully type-correct at all layers.

I also found it useful to put as many checks as possible into a separate pass that runs after the main type checking. For example, Futhark has restrictions on higher-order functions: you cannot return them from branches or loops. Previously, we would decorate pertinent type variables with such information, which complicated the core inference algorithm and sometimes resulted in hard-to-understand error messages. Now, we run through the program after type inference and look for any such forbidden uses. If any are found, it is easy to pinpoint the cause: you tell the user that this expression is not allowed, and this is the type we inferred for it.

One potential downside of this approach is inefficiency - it is usually more expensive to do four things than one thing. Further, some of these stages technically do more than one traversal of the function (in particular size inference) to check for various other problems or update AST decorations.

Now for an aside on compiler efficiency. Futhark was never intended to compile quickly. Personally, when I first started working on Futhark, I was inspired by such projects as Stalin and MLton, which were famous for combining unexpected high performance with very long compile times. My aspiration was for people to say of Futhark “it sure can generate fast code if you wait for it”. Perhaps I even intended to take a bit of pride in compile times being as impractical as Stalin, and it would certainly serve as a convenient excuse for why the language wasn’t used much. (Although while MLton is slow as compilers go, hardware improvements have made it quite practical these days.) However, since then Futhark has not only been used by (a few) other people; we also use it ourselves to conduct research into parallel programming techniques. It is a lot more fun to do this when you can get reasonably prompt feedback from the compiler, and hence while the Futhark type checker is certainly by most standards quite slow, we do try to some extent to not make it much slower than it currently is.

In my initial work on splitting the type checker, I observed a 20% slowdown of futhark check on a representative program, but I managed to get that down to about -4% (that is, a speedup) through various optimisations. Most of these were tedious tricks: checking whether some work was truly necessary before undertaking it. Could I have added such optimisations to the old type checker as well and thus made it even faster? Yes, but I didn’t. I’m not trying to make the fastest type checker, merely to not make it even slower. (But if someone wants to write fast type checker, please do reach out! I think the current design makes it fairly easy to benchmark components in isolation.)

As part of of this work, I also prepared the type checker to allow recursion (despite previous concerns), currently gated by the secret environment variable FUTHARK_ALLOW_RECURSION, but that is a story for a different day.

sentences.zh: 该实现是算法 W 的经典（有些人可能会说“老式”）实现；标准 Hindley-Milner 类型推理算法。

sentences.en: During traversal of a function, we immediately solved type constraints.
sentences.zh: 在遍历函数的过程中，我们立即解决了类型约束。

sentences.en: However, we had some type system features that were never a natural fit with this approach.
sentences.zh: 然而，我们有一些类型系统功能与这种方法并不自然匹配。

sentences.en: Both our record system, and also our uniqueness types, depended on being able to query the current type of some program fragment and make decisions based on it.
sentences.zh: 我们的记录系统以及唯一性类型都依赖于能够查询某些程序片段的当前类型并根据它做出决策。

sentences.en: This would show up in cases like branches, where the aliases of if a then b else c depended on whether the type of b and c can carry aliases (primitives cannot).
sentences.zh: 这会出现在分支之类的情况下，其中 if a then b else c 的别名取决于 b 和 c 的类型是否可以携带别名（基元不能）。

sentences.en: We managed to put together an implementation that worked by putting various extra information into our representation of type variables, but it was a little complicated, not obviously correct, and somewhat tedious to debug.
sentences.zh: 我们设法通过将各种额外信息放入类型变量的表示中来组合一个实现，但它有点复杂，不是明显正确的，并且调试起来有些乏味。

blocks.html_en: Later, when we added proper size types, we did it by extending the basic Hindley-Milner system by essentially also treating sizes as a kind of “type” subject to unification. At this point the system was beginning to creak. Consider again a branch if a then b else c - what is the size of the result? If b and c are arrays, we need to determine whether they have the same size, and if not, generate an existential for the result size. But at the point where we are looking at this if, we may not yet know whether they are arrays! Many similar cases occurred, and the type system had various restrictions and quirks where the well-typedness of a function would depend in somewhat subtle ways on whether you had put in type annotations. One nice property however was that if you put in type annotations on all parameters, which is generally considered good style anyway, then things worked pretty predictably. But still, the type checker was not particularly fun to hack on at this point.

blocks.html_en: Eventually, this design - a single program traversal manipulating an increasingly complicated state - stopped being workable. This was when we started working on AUTOMAP, a (not yet finished) system that loosely means that instead of saying map f x, you can just say f x and have the compiler figure out how many maps are needed. During this work, we discovered that AUTOMAP had to work by taking a global view of a function, typechecking it partially but without inferring specific sizes of arrays, determining the smallest number of maps needed to make it typecheck (by using an ILP solver), and then determine specific sizes. There was no way to fit this design into the existing type checker, so I began taking it apart into multiple phases.

sentences.en: Eventually, this design - a single program traversal manipulating an increasingly complicated state - stopped being workable.
sentences.zh: 最终，这种设计（单个程序遍历操纵日益复杂的状态）不再可行。

sentences.en: This was when we started working on AUTOMAP, a (not yet finished) system that loosely means that instead of saying map f x, you can just say f x and have the compiler figure out how many maps are needed.

sentences.en: During this work, we discovered that AUTOMAP had to work by taking a global view of a function, typechecking it partially but without inferring specific sizes of arrays, determining the smallest number of maps needed to make it typecheck (by using an ILP solver), and then determine specific sizes.
sentences.zh: 在这项工作中，我们发现 AUTOMAP 必须通过以下方式工作：获取函数的全局视图，对其进行部分类型检查，但不推断数组的具体大小，确定进行类型检查所需的最小映射数量（通过使用 ILP 求解器），然后确定具体大小。

sentences.en: There was no way to fit this design into the existing type checker, so I began taking it apart into multiple phases.
sentences.zh: 无法将此设计融入现有的类型检查器中，因此我开始将其分解为多个阶段。

blocks.html_en: The first phase I extracted was name resolution. It does what you would expect: resolves every name in the program, in practice by assigning each a unique identifier. This was a small win, but it simplified the type checker a little. The next phase was the conceptually final one: checking for aliases and in-place update violations. This turned out to be much more profitable, as this machinery had always been hindered by having to work with incomplete type information. Now it would be given a fully well-typed program and simply had to figure out whether any of the in-place constraints were violated (essentially, whether in-place updates are semantically observable).

sentences.en: The first phase I extracted was name resolution.

sentences.en: It does what you would expect: resolves every name in the program, in practice by assigning each a unique identifier.

sentences.en: This turned out to be much more profitable, as this machinery had always been hindered by having to work with incomplete type information.
sentences.zh: 事实证明，这样做的利润要高得多，因为这种机制一直因必须处理不完整的类型信息而受到阻碍。

sentences.en: Now it would be given a fully well-typed program and simply had to figure out whether any of the in-place constraints were violated (essentially, whether in-place updates are semantically observable).
sentences.zh: 现在，它将得到一个完全类型良好的程序，并且只需弄清楚是否违反了任何就地约束（本质上，就地更新在语义上是否可观察到）。

blocks.html_en: We were now left with a remaining core piece: a “term type checker” that did both normal ML-style type inference, but also size inference. As an expediency, to make the AUTOMAP research project progress, I did a hacky thing: I wrote an entirely new constraint-based type checker that did not perform size inference, but rather only standard type inference. We call this the unsized type checker. Instead of solving type equations on the fly, it generated a set of type constraints (mostly type equalities), which were solved “offline” by a constraint solver. This “generate-and-solve” approach makes handling complicated type system features more tractable as you don’t have to make decisions based on partial information, and is also used by the Glasgow Haskell Compiler and the Flix. In our implementation, we used these constraints to drive the AUTOMAP solver, then threw away those constraints and re-ran the original size-aware type checker (along with a bit of extra information provided by AUTOMAP). This worked, in that all programs could type check, but I think our paper puts it generously when it says “our Automap-enabled type checker is unoptimized”. My concern was not merely the inefficiency of redundant work, but the lack of clarity by duplicated logic - every type checking rule was duplicated in code!

blocks.html_en: I still have some hope for AUTOMAP itself, but getting it in a workable state requires an unknown amount of implementation effort. However, during this work I had become attracted to the elegance of constraint-based type checking. The central advantage is that you can postpone decisions until all information is available. From a human perspective, it is also much easier to debug (or benchmark) when you can print out all the constraints and reason about them. Unfortunately, our implementation was abominable. Last year I started work on doing constraint-based type checking without AUTOMAP, and in particular where duplication of logic was minimised. The idea was to use the unsized type checker to infer “ground” types (meaning types without filling in the specific sizes of arrays), then do a second pass that performs only size inference.

blocks.html_en: In the old type checker, this was done with some clever (oh no…) programming deep in the middle of unification whenever it encountered flexible size-lifted type variables. Since the unsized type checker has now determined all ground types in advance, no flexible type variables are ever encountered, so I had to work out a different solution. The details are too intricate to get into here, but they are ultimately a more explicit handling of the issue. There are still some ugly parts where I think we never worked out a good theory for size inference, but the part of the code that they infect is now a lot smaller than it used to be. I still have a vague idea that we should redo all of size checking at one point, perhaps basing it on bidirectional type checking instead of full inference, since it really is a dependent type system.

blocks.html_en: One lovely property of this design is that if an error is encountered in stage i, we have full information from stage i-1 to help explain the error. This is a nice change from the original single-pass type checker, where if something went wrong, we would usually have no information about those parts of the program that we have yet to traverse. I suspect a general principle that it’s a good idea to design a type system in “layers”, with each adding more restrictions to the former, such that a program’s meaning can be understood even when it is not fully type-correct at all layers.

sentences.en: One lovely property of this design is that if an error is encountered in stage i, we have full information from stage i-1 to help explain the error.
sentences.zh: 这种设计的一个可爱的特性是，如果在第 i 阶段遇到错误，我们可以从第 i-1 阶段获得完整信息来帮助解释错误。

sentences.en: This is a nice change from the original single-pass type checker, where if something went wrong, we would usually have no information about those parts of the program that we have yet to traverse.

sentences.en: I suspect a general principle that it’s a good idea to design a type system in “layers”, with each adding more restrictions to the former, such that a program’s meaning can be understood even when it is not fully type-correct at all layers.
sentences.zh: 我怀疑一个普遍原则，即在“层”中设计类型系统是一个好主意，每个层都对前者添加更多限制，这样即使程序在所有层上的类型不完全正确，也可以理解它的含义。

blocks.html_en: I also found it useful to put as many checks as possible into a separate pass that runs after the main type checking. For example, Futhark has restrictions on higher-order functions: you cannot return them from branches or loops. Previously, we would decorate pertinent type variables with such information, which complicated the core inference algorithm and sometimes resulted in hard-to-understand error messages. Now, we run through the program after type inference and look for any such forbidden uses. If any are found, it is easy to pinpoint the cause: you tell the user that this expression is not allowed, and this is the type we inferred for it.

blocks.html_en: One potential downside of this approach is inefficiency - it is usually more expensive to do four things than one thing. Further, some of these stages technically do more than one traversal of the function (in particular size inference) to check for various other problems or update AST decorations.

sentences.en: One potential downside of this approach is inefficiency - it is usually more expensive to do four things than one thing.
sentences.zh: 这种方法的一个潜在缺点是效率低下——做四件事通常比做一件事更昂贵。

sentences.en: Further, some of these stages technically do more than one traversal of the function (in particular size inference) to check for various other problems or update AST decorations.
sentences.zh: 此外，其中一些阶段在技术上会执行多次函数遍历（特别是大小推断），以检查各种其他问题或更新 AST 装饰。

blocks.html_en: Now for an aside on compiler efficiency. Futhark was never intended to compile quickly. Personally, when I first started working on Futhark, I was inspired by such projects as Stalin and MLton, which were famous for combining unexpected high performance with very long compile times. My aspiration was for people to say of Futhark “it sure can generate fast code if you wait for it”. Perhaps I even intended to take a bit of pride in compile times being as impractical as Stalin, and it would certainly serve as a convenient excuse for why the language wasn’t used much. (Although while MLton is slow as compilers go, hardware improvements have made it quite practical these days.) However, since then Futhark has not only been used by (a few) other people; we also use it ourselves to conduct research into parallel programming techniques. It is a lot more fun to do this when you can get reasonably prompt feedback from the compiler, and hence while the Futhark type checker is certainly by most standards quite slow, we do try to some extent to not make it much slower than it currently is.

sentences.en: Now for an aside on compiler efficiency.
sentences.zh: 现在谈谈编译器效率。

sentences.en: Futhark was never intended to compile quickly.
sentences.zh: Futhark 从未打算快速编译。

sentences.en: Personally, when I first started working on Futhark, I was inspired by such projects as Stalin and MLton, which were famous for combining unexpected high performance with very long compile times.
sentences.zh: 就我个人而言，当我第一次开始研究 Futhark 时，我受到了 Stalin 和 MLton 等项目的启发，这些项目以将意想不到的高性能与超长编译时间相结合而闻名。

sentences.en: My aspiration was for people to say of Futhark “it sure can generate fast code if you wait for it”.
sentences.zh: 我的愿望是让人们对 Futhark 说“如果你等待它，它肯定可以生成快速的代码”。

sentences.en: Perhaps I even intended to take a bit of pride in compile times being as impractical as Stalin, and it would certainly serve as a convenient excuse for why the language wasn’t used much.
sentences.zh: 也许我什至想为编译时间像斯大林一样不切实际而感到自豪，这肯定可以作为为什么该语言没有被广泛使用的一个方便的借口。

sentences.en: (Although while MLton is slow as compilers go, hardware improvements have made it quite practical these days.)
sentences.zh: （尽管随着编译器的发展，MLton 的速度很慢，但硬件的改进使其如今变得非常实用。）

sentences.en: However, since then Futhark has not only been used by (a few) other people; we also use it ourselves to conduct research into parallel programming techniques.
sentences.zh: 然而，从那时起，Futhark 不仅被（少数）其他人使用；而且还被其他人使用。我们自己也用它来研究并行编程技术。

sentences.en: It is a lot more fun to do this when you can get reasonably prompt feedback from the compiler, and hence while the Futhark type checker is certainly by most standards quite slow, we do try to some extent to not make it much slower than it currently is.
sentences.zh: 当您可以从编译器获得合理及时的反馈时，这样做会更有趣，因此，虽然 Futhark 类型检查器按照大多数标准肯定相当慢，但我们确实在某种程度上尝试不让它比当前慢很多。

blocks.html_en: In my initial work on splitting the type checker, I observed a 20% slowdown of futhark check on a representative program, but I managed to get that down to about -4% (that is, a speedup) through various optimisations. Most of these were tedious tricks: checking whether some work was truly necessary before undertaking it. Could I have added such optimisations to the old type checker as well and thus made it even faster? Yes, but I didn’t. I’m not trying to make the fastest type checker, merely to not make it even slower. (But if someone wants to write fast type checker, please do reach out! I think the current design makes it fairly easy to benchmark components in isolation.)

sentences.en: In my initial work on splitting the type checker, I observed a 20% slowdown of futhark check on a representative program, but I managed to get that down to about -4% (that is, a speedup) through various optimisations.
sentences.zh: 在我最初拆分类型检查器的工作中，我观察到代表性程序上的 futhark 检查速度减慢了 20%，但我设法通过各种优化将其降低到约 -4%（即加速）。

sentences.zh: 其中大多数都是乏味的技巧：在进行某些工作之前检查它是否确实必要。

sentences.en: Yes, but I didn’t.

sentences.zh: （但是如果有人想编写快速类型检查器，请联系我们！

sentences.zh: 我认为当前的设计使得单独对组件进行基准测试变得相当容易。）

blocks.html_en: As part of of this work, I also prepared the type checker to allow recursion (despite previous concerns), currently gated by the secret environment variable FUTHARK_ALLOW_RECURSION, but that is a story for a different day.

sentences.en: As part of of this work, I also prepared the type checker to allow recursion (despite previous concerns), currently gated by the secret environment variable FUTHARK_ALLOW_RECURSION, but that is a story for a different day.
sentences.zh: 作为这项工作的一部分，我还准备了类型检查器以允许递归（尽管之前有担心），目前由秘密环境变量 FUTHARK_ALLOW_RECURSION 控制，但那是另一天的故事了。

content_html_zh:
发表于 2026 年 7 月 21 日

这篇文章是关于 Futhark 类型检查器的演变，其动机是我即将合并的大型重构。 它可能是其他语言设计者最感兴趣的，并且包含一些我希望在我们刚开始时就知道的课程 - 尽管我对类型检查文献不是特别了解，所以所有这些可能都是旧帽子。

背景

早在我们第一次设计 Futhark 的原始时代，类型系统就很简单：唯一的类型是标量、数组和元组，所有函数都是一阶和单态的，并且没有类型推断。 所有函数都必须用其参数和返回类型进行注释。 从（近）开始出现的唯一奇特功能是通过一种唯一性类型进行就地更新，尽管当时我们并没有真正理解它们有多么棘手。

那时它实际上只是一个类型检查器：从我们被告知的事实（参数类型）开始，我们检查表达式的类型是否一致，并跟踪别名以验证就地更新的使用。 该实现是对每个函数进行简单的自上而下的单次遍历，在此期间，AST 用其确定的类型装饰节点，然后由后续传递使用。 它与我在编程语言实现的标准课程中教过的类型检查器（并且自学以来）完全相同，当您可以摆脱它时，这是一个很好的设计：易于实现，非常高效，易于理解。 当时我们真的不理解正确检查安全使用就地更新的规则的复杂性，因此这种简单性可能是虚幻的，但那时我们仍然很高兴。

当然，我们并没有逃脱太久。 我们最终开始添加记录和 ML 风格的模块系统等功能，到 0.4.0 版本，我们引入了高阶函数和 Hindley-Milner 风格的类型推断。 该实现是算法 W 的经典（有些人可能会说“老式”）实现；标准 Hindley-Milner 类型推理算法。 在遍历函数的过程中，我们立即解决了类型约束。 然而，我们有一些类型系统功能与这种方法并不自然匹配。 我们的记录系统以及唯一性类型都依赖于能够查询某些程序片段的当前类型并根据它做出决策。 这会出现在分支之类的情况下，其中 if a then b else c 的别名取决于 b 和 c 的类型是否可以携带别名（基元不能）。 我们设法通过将各种额外信息放入类型变量的表示中来组合一个实现，但它有点复杂，不是明显正确的，并且调试起来有些乏味。

后来，当我们添加适当的尺寸类型时，我们通过扩展基本的 Hindley-Milner 系统来做到这一点，本质上也将尺寸视为一种需要统一的“类型”。 就在这时，系统开始嘎吱作响。 再次考虑一个分支 if a then b else c - 结果的大小是多少？ 如果 b 和 c 是数组，我们需要确定它们是否具有相同的大小，如果不同，则为结果大小生成一个存在式。 但当我们看到这个 if 时，我们可能还不知道它们是否是数组！ 许多类似的情况发生了，并且类型系统有各种限制和怪癖，其中函数的类型良好会以某种微妙的方式取决于您是否已放入类型注释。 然而，一个很好的特性是，如果您在所有参数上添加类型注释（无论如何这通常被认为是良好的风格），那么事情就会按预期进行。 但是，此时使用类型检查器并不是特别有趣。

最终，这种设计（单个程序遍历操纵日益复杂的状态）不再可行。 这是我们开始研究 AUTOMAP 的时候，这是一个（尚未完成的）系统，大致意味着您可以只说 f x，而不是说 map f x，然后让编译器计算出需要多少个映射。 在这项工作中，我们发现 AUTOMAP 必须通过以下方式工作：获取函数的全局视图，对其进行部分类型检查，但不推断数组的具体大小，确定进行类型检查所需的最小映射数量（通过使用 ILP 求解器），然后确定具体大小。 无法将此设计融入现有的类型检查器中，因此我开始将其分解为多个阶段。

我提取的第一个阶段是名称解析。 它执行您所期望的操作：实际上通过为每个名称分配一个唯一的标识符来解析程序中的每个名称。 这是一个小小的胜利，但它稍微简化了类型检查器。 下一阶段是概念上的最后阶段：检查别名和就地更新违规。 事实证明，这样做的利润要高得多，因为这种机制一直因必须处理不完整的类型信息而受到阻碍。 现在，它将得到一个完全类型良好的程序，并且只需弄清楚是否违反了任何就地约束（本质上，就地更新在语义上是否可观察到）。

现在我们剩下一个核心部分：一个“术语类型检查器”，它既可以进行正常的 ML 风格的类型推断，也可以进行大小推断。 作为权宜之计，为了使 AUTOMAP 研究项目取得进展，我做了一件很奇怪的事情：我编写了一个全新的基于约束的类型检查器，它不执行大小推断，而只执行标准类型推断。 我们称之为未调整大小的类型检查器。 它不是即时求解类型方程，而是生成一组类型约束（主要是类型等式），并由约束求解器“离线”求解。 这种“生成并解决”的方法使处理复杂的类型系统功能变得更容易处理，因为您不必根据部分信息做出决策，并且格拉斯哥 Haskell 编译器和 Flix 也使用这种方法。 在我们的实现中，我们使用这些约束来驱动 AUTOMAP 求解器，然后丢弃这些约束并重新运行原始的大小感知类型检查器（以及 AUTOMAP 提供的一些额外信息）。 这很有效，因为所有程序都可以进行类型检查，但我认为我们的论文在说“我们启用 Automap 的类型检查器未优化”时慷慨地表达了这一点。 我担心的不仅仅是冗余工作的低效率，而是重复逻辑缺乏清晰度——每个类型检查规则都在代码中重复！

新的类型检查器

我对 AUTOMAP 本身仍然抱有一些希望，但要使其处于可用状态需要未知数量的实施工作。 然而，在这项工作中，我被基于约束的类型检查的优雅所吸引。 核心优势是您可以推迟决策，直到所有信息都可用为止。 从人类的角度来看，当您可以打印出所有约束和推理时，调试（或基准测试）也会容易得多。 不幸的是，我们的实施情况很糟糕。 去年，我开始在不使用 AUTOMAP 的情况下进行基于约束的类型检查，特别是在逻辑重复最小化的情况下。 这个想法是使用未调整大小的类型检查器来推断“基础”类型（意味着不填充数组的特定大小的类型），然后进行第二遍仅执行大小推断。

主要的障碍，也是我直到现在才完成的原因，源于与存在大小相关的棘手情况，其中数组大小的存在量词必须插入到正确的位置。 考虑一个具有大小提升类型变量的函数，如下所示：

def apply 'a '^b (f: a -> b) (x: a) =
  f x

由于 ^b 是大小提升的，因此可以使用包含存在量词的类型来实例化它，在这种情况下意味着 f 可以是返回未知大小的数组的函数。

如果我们说 apply iota，那么未调整大小的类型检查器将为 apply 推断出此类型：

(f: i64 -> []i64) -> (x: i64) -> []i64 =

当我们执行大小推断时，推断这种类型非常重要，在返回类型中使用存在量化 (?[n])：

(f: i64 -> ?[n].[n]i64) -> (x: i64) -> ?[m].[m]i64

而不是让返回大小作为大小参数一直浮动到顶部：

(f: i64 -> [n]i64) -> (x: i64) -> [n]i64

这个不正确的类型表明 f 返回的数组在调用 f 之前是已知的，并且不依赖于它的参数。 对于某些 fs 是这样，但当 f=iota 时则不然。

在旧的类型检查器中，这是通过一些聪明的方法完成的（哦不......） 每当遇到灵活的大小提升类型变量时，都会在统一过程中进行深入编程。 由于未调整大小的类型检查器现在已经提前确定了所有基础类型，因此不会遇到灵活的类型变量，因此我必须制定不同的解决方案。 细节太复杂，无法在这里展开，但它们最终是对问题的更明确的处理。 仍然有一些丑陋的部分，我认为我们从未制定出一个好的大小推断理论，但它们感染的代码部分现在比以前小很多。 我仍然有一个模糊的想法，我们应该在某一时刻重做所有大小检查，也许基于双向类型检查而不是完全推理，因为它确实是一个依赖类型系统。

但这是为了未来。 通过这项工作，Futhark 类型检查器现在为每个函数经历四个阶段：

  1. 解决名字。
  2. 使用未调整大小的类型检查器确定地面类型。
  3. 执行尺寸推断。
  4. 检查就地更新违规情况。

（如果我们完成了 AUTOMAP，它将在阶段 2 和阶段 3 之间进行。）

这种设计的一个可爱的特性是，如果在第 i 阶段遇到错误，我们可以从第 i-1 阶段获得完整信息来帮助解释错误。 这是对原始单遍类型检查器的一个很好的改变，如果出现问题，我们通常不会获得有关程序中尚未遍历的那些部分的信息。 我怀疑一个普遍原则，即在“层”中设计类型系统是一个好主意，每个层都对前者添加更多限制，这样即使程序在所有层上的类型不完全正确，也可以理解它的含义。

我还发现将尽可能多的检查放入在主要类型检查之后运行的单独传递中很有用。 例如，Futhark 对高阶函数有限制：不能从分支或循环返回它们。 以前，我们会用这些信息来装饰相关的类型变量，这使核心推理算法变得复杂，有时会导致难以理解的错误消息。 现在，我们在类型推断后运行程序并查找任何此类禁止的用途。 如果发现任何情况，很容易查明原因：您告诉用户不允许使用此表达式，这就是我们为其推断的类型。

这种方法的一个潜在缺点是效率低下——做四件事通常比做一件事更昂贵。 此外，其中一些阶段在技术上会执行多次函数遍历（特别是大小推断），以检查各种其他问题或更新 AST 装饰。

现在谈谈编译器效率。 Futhark 从未打算快速编译。 就我个人而言，当我第一次开始研究 Futhark 时，我受到了 Stalin 和 MLton 等项目的启发，这些项目以将意想不到的高性能与超长编译时间相结合而闻名。 我的愿望是让人们对 Futhark 说“如果你等待它，它肯定可以生成快速的代码”。 也许我什至想为编译时间像斯大林一样不切实际而感到自豪，这肯定可以作为为什么该语言没有被广泛使用的一个方便的借口。 （尽管随着编译器的发展，MLton 的速度很慢，但硬件的改进使其如今变得非常实用。） 然而，从那时起，Futhark 不仅被（少数）其他人使用；而且还被其他人使用。我们自己也用它来研究并行编程技术。 当您可以从编译器获得合理及时的反馈时，这样做会更有趣，因此，虽然 Futhark 类型检查器按照大多数标准肯定相当慢，但我们确实在某种程度上尝试不让它比当前慢很多。

在我最初拆分类型检查器的工作中，我观察到代表性程序上的 futhark 检查速度减慢了 20%，但我设法通过各种优化将其降低到约 -4%（即加速）。 其中大多数都是乏味的技巧：在进行某些工作之前检查它是否确实必要。 我是否可以向旧的类型检查器添加这样的优化，从而使其更快？ 是的，但我没有。 我并不是想制作最快的类型检查器，只是为了不让它变得更慢。 （但是如果有人想编写快速类型检查器，请联系我们！ 我认为当前的设计使得单独对组件进行基准测试变得相当容易。）

作为这项工作的一部分，我还准备了类型检查器以允许递归（尽管之前有担心），目前由秘密环境变量 FUTHARK_ALLOW_RECURSION 控制，但那是另一天的故事了。
